coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-combined ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe based on biomass-derived carbon dots for determination of tyramine in fermented meat products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dianwei
Zhang, Yuhua
Li, Kexin
Wang, Shengnan
Ma, Yuanchen
Liao, Yonghong
Wang, Fenghuan
Liu, Huilin
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe employing two distinct emission wavelengths of biomass carbon dots was developed for highly selective and visual quantitative detection of tyramine in fermented meat products. The red emission biomass carbon dots were employed as responsive elements, and the blue ones were utilized as the reference elements. The molecularly imprinted polymers were incorporated in the ratiometric sensing to distinguish and adsorb tyramine. With the linear range of 1–60 μg/L, the ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe was successfully applied to detect tyramine in real samples with the satisfactory recoveries of 79.74–112.12% and the detect limitation of 1.3 μg/kg, indicating that this probe has great potential applications for the detection of tyramine in real samples. Moreover, smartphone-based fluorescence signal recognition analysis on hand has been developed for the quantitative analysis of tyramine, providing a portable visual optical analysis terminal for rapid on-site determination of tyramine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tyramine
Biomass-derived carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Fermented meat products

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of b-BCDs, r-BCDs, and r/b-BCDs@MIPs
Selectivity, competitiveness, and interference analysis of r/b-BCDs@MIPs
Detection of tyramine in food samples
Synthesis and optimization of BCDs, r/b-BCDs@MIPs and tyramine detection mechanism
Detection of tyramine in food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFF1101101) and the Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBU).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the b-BCDs with a series of excitation wavelengths. (B) UV absorption spectrum of the b-BCDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the r-BCDs with a series of excitation wavelengths. (D) UV absorption spectrum of the r-BCDs. (E) UV absorption spectra of BCDs, tyramine, r-BCDs with tyramine and emission spectrum of BCDs. (F) Transient fluorescence spectrum of BCDs and r-BCDs in the presence of tyramine.

(A) TEM image of b-BCDs. (B) TEM image of r-BCDs. (C) TEM image of MIPs. (D) SEM image of r/b-BCDs@MIPs. (E) TEM image of r/b-BCDs@NIPs. (F) SEM image of r/b-BCDs@NIPs (G) FT-IR spectra of BCDs. (H) FT-IR spectra of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and r/b-BCDs@NIPs.

(A) Effect of solvent on the performance of r/b-BCDs@MIPs. (B) Adsorption time of r/b-BCDs@MIPs. (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and the concentration of tyramine. (D) Fluorescence responses of the r/b-BCDs@MIPs in the presence of tyramine at various concentrations (1–60 μg/L). (E) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of r/b-BCDs@NIPs and the tyramine concentration. (F) Fluorescence responses of the r/b-BCDs@NIPs in the presence of tyramine at various concentrations (1–60 μg/L).
(A) Schematic of the smartphone readout analyzer based on r/b-BCDs@MIPs to detect tyramine. (B) Linear relationship between B/R and concentration of tyramine.
Fabrication process of BCDs and r/b-BCDs@MIPs.
Detection and recoveries of spiked tyramine in fermented meat products samples by r/b-BCDs@MIPs.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-combined ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe based on biomass-derived carbon dots for determination of tyramine in fermented meat products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China.
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe employing two distinct emission wavelengths of biomass carbon dots was developed for highly selective and visual quantitative detection of tyramine in fermented meat products. The red emission biomass carbon dots were employed as responsive elements, and the blue ones were utilized as the reference elements. The molecularly imprinted polymers were incorporated in the ratiometric sensing to distinguish and adsorb tyramine. With the linear range of 1–60 μg/L, the ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe was successfully applied to detect tyramine in real samples with the satisfactory recoveries of 79.74–112.12% and the detect limitation of 1.3 μg/kg, indicating that this probe has great potential applications for the detection of tyramine in real samples. Moreover, smartphone-based fluorescence signal recognition analysis on hand has been developed for the quantitative analysis of tyramine, providing a portable visual optical analysis terminal for rapid on-site determination of tyramine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tyramine, p-hydroxyl phenylethylamine, is a low molecular mass aromatic biogenic amine that is widely present in living organisms and a variety of foods, especially in fermented foods (Luo et al., 2022). It is mainly produced from tyrosine through the decarboxylation process by tyrosine decarboxylase during fermentation and decomposition. Tyramine, as a neuromodulator, plays a very important role in regulating the nervous system and has profound effects on various bodily functions (Tırıs et al., 2023; D. Zhang, Liu, Geng, & Wang, 2019). Nevertheless, excessive intake of tyramine often leads to headaches, dizziness, and other adverse effects (Dala-Paula, Custódio, & Gloria, 2023; Vasconcelos et al., 2021). In addition, it has been shown that tyramine can also be used as a marker of food spoilage, and variations in its concentration can be used to determine the freshness of food (da Silva et al., 2019; De Mey et al., 2014; Pratibha, Kapoor, & Rajput, 2022). Currently, various food regulatory bodies, including the EFSA (European Food Safety Association) and FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations), have declared tyramine to be potentially hazardous in foods (Hu, Huang, Li, & Yang, 2012; Pinto, Nieto, Zón, Fernández, & de Araujo, 2016). Consequently, there is an urgent need for sensitive, precise, highly selective, rapid and easy-to-handle methods to detect and quantify tyramine concentrations in foods.

The content of tyramine in foods is frequently estimated by derivatization coupled with large-scale instruments, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography (GC) and chromatography-mass spectrometry, ect. (Kamankesh, Mohammadi, Mollahosseini, & Seidi, 2019; Xu et al., 2023). These methodologies have been broadly employed in numerous large-scale testing organizations or laboratories, offering precision and reproducibility, nevertheless the analysis procedure is extremely inconvenient, laborious, and expensive, therefore, making it problematic to accomplish real-time and on-site detection. In recent years, a succession of fluorescence imprinted probes based on nanomaterials for tyramine detection have been established, having the distinguished advantages in terms of rapidity, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness (Teepoo, Promta, & Phapugrangkul, 2019; Wang, Lu, Wang, & Chen, 2019).

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) represent a distinct kind of biomimetic antibodies, capable of binding to the particular target by means of hydrogen bonding facilitating targeted adsorption, with precise selectivity and anti-interference advantages (Amiripour, Ghasemi, & Azizi, 2021; Arabi et al., 2022; Arabi & Chen, 2022; Meng, Chen, Zhang, Liu, & Sun, 2024; Zhu, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2022). Although MIPs have good selectivity, they lack signal transmission capabilities in analytical and detection applications. Therefore, some reported studies have introduced fluorescent materials into molecular imprinting systems, such as fluorescent dyes, quantum dots, carbon dots, and metal frame materials, etc., utilizing fluorescence signals combined with molecular imprinting technology to achieve selective recognition and detection of target molecules (Chumkaeo, Davis, & Bunkoed, 2024; Seangyai, Chansud, & Bunkoed, 2023). However, these methodologies are susceptible to changes by numerous aspects including background noises, matrix effects, probe concentration, light source, and instrument interference (Peng, Yang, Zhang, & Jia, 2022; Yang et al., 2024). To prevail over these above-mentioned challenges and progress the precision of analyte detection in complex systems, the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted probes have grown considerable attention, which can mitigate the errors and modify the detection results through gauging the ratio of two emission peaks at different wavelengths (Fu et al., 2022; Pei et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2022). The preparation and optimization of MIPs selectivity have always been a concern, and the toxicity of MIPs during the preparation process, the recovery life cycle, and the green treatment of MIPs recovery are also issues for the wide application of MIPs (Arabi et al., 2021; Ostovan et al., 2022).

The advancement of luminescent materials has encouraged the expansion of ratiometric fluorescence imprinted probes (Guo, Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2021). Carbon dots (CDs) represent a distinctive category of luminescent nanomaterial predominantly comprised of carbon elements. These were primarily discovered serendipitously in 2004 and had steadily been applied in innumerable findings since then (Chang et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2023). Presently, immense investigations have been carried out to use biomass waste as a sole source of carbon to prepare biomass-derived CDs (BCDs). Due to enormous characteristics of biomass waste being eco-friendly, renewable, low-cost, accessible, and benign in nature, it is used as a carbon source for the production of BCDs. BCDs have been extensively used in numerous fields owing to its wide range of applications including sensors, imaging, and drug delivery for the excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, stability, and easy-to-modify surface groups (Han, Zhu, Liu, & Sun, 2022; Zhang et al., 2023; Zhang, Zhang, Liao, Wang, & Liu, 2022). The utilization of biomass waste to formulate BCDs can transform low-value biomass waste into high-value materials.

Herein, a newly developed ratiometric fluorescence imprinted probe (r/b-BCDs@MIPs), based on r-BCDs and b-BCDs, had been constructed for the detection of tyramine. These both BCDs with distinct emission wavelengths were synthesized from the same biomass waste by using hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, correspondingly. The ratio of different emission peak intensities (F670/F460) could be used as a detecting indicator for sensitive detection of tyramine. The r/b-BCDs@MIPs based ratiometric sensing of tyramine was efficaciously attained in fermented meat products with satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) using the spiking recovery method. Furthermore, the outcomes were confirmed by carrying out the HPLC method for verifying its reliability. In conclusion, to further employ the benefits of optical sensing and accomplish the demands for rapid detection, a tyramine detection method based on smartphone image analysis was devised by employing universal smartphone software for image analysis. Therefore, the constructed probe exhibited sensitivity and selectivity for tyramine detection and facilitated convenient sensing approach for visual sensing of tyramine. Compared with traditional single emission probes, the developed r/b-BCDs@MIPs displayed high sensitivity, sufficient color change, self-calibration characteristics, and visual analysis.

The main primary synthetic reagents, including methacrylic acid (MAA), 2, 2′-Azobis ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), as well as the standard samples of tyramine, tyrosine, tryptamine, tryptophan, histamine, and histidine were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetone was sourced from Modern Oriental Fine Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All solvents and other reagents utilized were analytical grade and purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was attained in house from the water purifier available in laboratory (Beijing, China). The passion fruits and samples of various fermented meat products were purchased through online means.

The morphological features of the samples were observed detected through a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU 8000, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, Thermo Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) images were documented by using ALPHA II (Bruker, Germany). XPS analysis was measured by Thermo scalable 250XI(Thermo Scientific, USA). XRD analysis was measured by D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany). TG and DTG analysis were performed by TG/DSC 3+ (Mettler Toledo, Swiss Confederation). Ultraviolet spectrophotometry was attained by UV-2700i spectrometer approach (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis of nanomaterials was carried out with a multifunctional enzyme labeling instrument (Synergy H1, Biotek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of b-BCDs, r-BCDs, and r/b-BCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Blue emission biomass carbon dots (b-BCDs) and red emission biomass carbon dots (r-BCDs) were produced from the same biomass waste material. In it for the synthesis of blue emission carbon dots 0.3 g of passion fruit shells powder was mixed with 30 mL of ultrapure water and then shifted to an autoclave reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. The precipitate was then centrifuged and removed at the end of the reaction when the reaction was cooled down to room temperature, and the b-BCDs were obtained from the supernatant by passing it through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The synthesis of r-BCDs was lightly different from that of b-BCDs. Initially, 1.5 g of passion fruit shells powder was mixed with 30 mL of acetone and transferred to an autoclave, and the reaction was carried out at 120 °C for 5 h. At the end of the reaction, the precipitate was removed by centrifugation and then the supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain a solution of r-BCDs.

The r/b-BCDs@MIPs were synthesized by the process of bulk polymerization. Primarily, 3.34 mL of r-BCDs ethanol solution was added into a 25 mL round-bottomed flask, and after shaking, the r-BCDs was consistently distributed in the system. Then, 0.2 mmol of tyramine and 0.6 mmol of MAA were added, and the solution was homogenized by ultrasonic treatment. Then the flask was placed in a 60 °C water bath and shaken for 30 min for pre-polymerization, and the flask was taken out. Secondly, 1.67 mL of b-BCDs and 2.4 mmol of EGDMA were added, and 30 mg of AIBN was added to initiate the polymerization reaction. The system was ultrasonicated again for homogenization and then placed at 60 °C for 24 h. Following the process of synthesis, the template was removed from the white solid product by Soxhlet extraction with methanol as the eluent, and the eluent was altered every 6 h. In conclusion, the template-removed material was placed in a drying oven for 5 h and systematically crushed to attain the r/b-BCDs@MIPs powder, which was stored in a light-proof place for future usage. As a reference, the r/b-BCDs@NIPs were prepared using the same procedure but without the template molecules for comparison with the r/b-BCDs@MIPs.

In the dynamic adsorption experiment, 0.5 mL of r/b-BCDs@MIPs ethanol solution (2 mg/mL) was mixed with an equal volume of 50 μg/L tyramine standard solution, and the excitation wavelength was fixed to 410 nm. The fluorescence response variation of the mixed system was monitored for 40 min. In the static adsorption experiment, 0.5 mL of r/b-BCDs@MIPs ethanol solution (2 mg/mL) and an equal amount of tyramine standard solution with a concentration range of 0.1–120 μg/L were mixed in a centrifuge tube. After incubation at room temperature for 1 h, 200 μL of the mixed solution was transferred to a 96-well microplate, and consequently the fluorescence spectrum was attained within the range of 440–700 nm under excitation at 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, competitiveness, and interference analysis of r/b-BCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: For selection and competitive experiments, various structural analogs of tyramine were employed including tyrosine, tryptamine, tryptophan, histamine, and histidine. The selectivity experimental procedure was consistent with static adsorption experiments. Competitive experiments used 5-, 10-, and 15-fold concentrations of competitors to evaluate the competitive effects. In the interference experiment, some potential interfering substances were nominated to further inspect the impact on the detection of tyramine by r/b-BCDs@MIPs.

The stability experiment of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was steered by simulating different investigating diverse concentrations of NaCl solutions with different ion concentrations to evaluate gauge their fluorescence intensity or signal stability. The anti-photobleaching performance and solution storage stability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs were also evaluated estimated, and the thermal stability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was evaluated assessed by using TG and DTG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tyramine in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three fermented meat samples were used additionally to assess the ability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs at three different concentrations (5, 15, 25 mg/kg), by using spiked recovery experiments. The results were confirmed by using HPLC methods, and the sample pre-treatment, extraction, and purification processes were explained in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

To further estimate the portability and visualization of the prepared sensing method, the fluorescence signals of the mixed system of r/b-BCDs@MIPs were read using a universal smartphone software, Spotxel® Reader (version 2.2.2)(Zhu, Yuan, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2021). The smartphone-based static adsorption experiments followed the same procedure as beforehand, excluding that after sufficiently oscillating the adsorption, the mixed system was placed in a dark box with a 365 nm UV lamp, and the images were captured and investigated using a smartphone based on the Spotxel® Reader software. MeanwhileIn the meantime, the actual samples and spiked recovery experiments were consistent with the previous established process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optimization of BCDs, r/b-BCDs@MIPs and tyramine detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In response to green chemistry and suitable attributes of carbon dots that includes low toxicity and preparation cost, the concept of synthesizing valuable fluorescent carbon dots from low-priced biomass wastes are progressively gaining consideration. In this experimentation, as exhibited in Scheme 1 , two distinct types of fluorescent carbon dots were manufactured from biomass waste by solvothermal synthesis and combined them with molecularly imprinted techniques to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of tyramine in fermented meat products. Different biomass waste materials including mangosteen shells, longan shells, passion fruit shells, dragon fruit peels, and mango peels were used to formulate the carbon dots. Remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of different biomass-derived carbon dots prepared using the same procedure and methodology varied significantly (Fig. S1A-E). At the same concentration (Fig. S1F), the significance of carbon dots synthesized from passion fruit shells were higher than those synthesized from the four biomass-derived carbon dots. Hence, passion fruit shells were nominated as a high-quality precursor material for synthesizing carbon dots for succeeding experiments. By optimizing the three conditions of temperature, time, and dosage for the one-pot hydrothermal method, the synthesis conditions of b-BCDs were finally determined (Fig. S1GI), and the optimum emission peak of b-BCDs attained as shown in Fig. 1A was situated near 450 nm, and the UV–vis spectrum of b-BCDs (Fig. 1B) exhibited that it had an absorption shoulder peak located at 280 nm, which could be from the b-BCDs surface CO group of n-π* (Xu, Gan, et al., 2023). Then, we used acetone instead of water for the solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots, and r-BCDs were gained by simply altering the reaction conditions, and it was fascinating to note that carbon dots synthesized by solvothermal synthesis had a long-wavelength emission (Fig. 1C), which was also exhibited by the UV–vis spectra of r-BCDs (Fig. 1D) to have a wider absorption region and had diverse absorption peaks at 230 nm, 270 nm and 665 nm as well as a shoulder peak at 340 nm. The relative quantum yields of b-BCDs and r-BCDs were calculated to be 3.78% and 2.44% using quinine sulfate and rhodamine B as reference substances, individually (Rao et al., 2020).

A red-blue ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed for the detection of trace tyramine using these two carbon dots. r-BCDs, tyramine, and MAA were pre-polymerized in a single step first, and the -COOH of MAA interacted with the -NH3 of tyramine through hydrogen bonding, and then b-BCDs were introduced as an internal reference, and the double-bonded polymerization of EGDMA was triggered by AIBN to guarantee that the aforementioned. Then the prepolymers and b-BCDs were encapsulated to construct the ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensing materials. The removal of tyramine was achieved through soxhlet extraction, establishing a huge number of recognition sites in the three-dimensional cavity on the surface of the r/b-BCDs@MIPs to precisely adsorb tyramine. To additionally examine the fluorescence quenching mechanism, experiments were carried out to validate the mechanism through the variations in UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetimes. As revealed in Fig. 1E, afterwards the addition of tyramine, the absorption peak of r-BCDs did not undergo any significant blue or red shift. It suggested that there was no formation of a new substance after mixing of r-BCDs and tyramine. At the same time, there was no overlapping part of the UV–vis spectra of tyramine and the fluorescence spectra of r-BCDs, thus excluding that the fluorescence quenching originated from resonance energy transfer Additionally, there was practically no modification in the fluorescence decay curve of r-BCDs before (7.54 ns) and after the addition of tyramine (7.42 ns) (Fig. 1F), which led us to conclude that this variation in fluorescence intensity was because of photoinduced electron transfer.

To achieve the finest ratio and intensity of fluorescence emission peaks as well as the greatest yield, the experiments were optimized by altering the ratio of r-BCDs to b-BCDs and the quantity of cross-linking agent (EGDMA) during the synthesis of r/b-BCDs@MIPs. As displayed in Fig. S2A and B, the dosage ratio of r-BCDs and b-BCDs was transformed from 1:1 to 3:1, and the value of F 670/F 460 sustained to rise from 1.73 to 4.8. In order to acquire improved recognition sensitivity and to influence the underlying signals of the b-BCDs, the ratio of the two was therefore set at 2:1. In addition, recognizing that the quantity of crosslinking agent straightly affected the polymerization degree and fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted material, the experiment augmented the amount of crosslinking agent EGDMA by fixing the ratio of template molecules and functional monomers. In addition, we optimized the dosage of functional monomer MAA and crosslinker EGDMA, as shown in Fig. S2C. The increase of MAA dosage helped to prepare MIPs for target adsorption, but when the dosage was greater than 1:3, the adsorption capacity of MIPs for targets was not significantly improved. As shown in Fig. S2D and E, when the amount of EGDMA increased from 20 mmol (1:3:10) to 40 mmol (1:3:20), the fluorescence intensity at 670 nm continued to decrease. This was because the increased dosage of EGDMA made the polymer encapsulation tighter, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Firstly, the ratio of F670/F460 rose to 4.22 and then declined. Nevertheless, the lower the dosage of EGDMA used, the lower the crosslinking degree of the polymer, resulting in a significant decrease in yield. Therefore, by combining yield, F670/F460, and fluorescence intensity, the optimal dose of 24 mmol EGDMA was designated, with a ratio of 1:3:12.

The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of b-BCDs and r-BCDs were revealed in Fig. 2A and B. Both b-BCDs and r-BCDs displayed homogeneously distributed particles, and no noticeable aggregation arisen in the solution. The particle size analysis software was employed to further regulate the particle size of the two carbon dots. As exhibited in Fig. 2A: inset, the average particle size of b-BCDs was around 2.98 nm, and its particle size distribution was larger, which was dependable on its excitation-dependent phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2B: inset, the average particle size of r-BCDs was approximately 2.34 nm, and its particle size distribution was moderately uniform. Fig. 2C and D illustrated the TEM and SEM images of r/b-BCDs@MIPs, correspondingly. Conferring to the images, the particle size of r/b-BCDs@MIPs had pointedly amplified related to the b-BCDs and the r-BCDs, displaying spherical nanoparticles that were mutually adhered, with an average particle size of approximately 213.97 nm (Fig. 2C: inset). For the purpose of comparison, the microstructural morphology of r/b-BCDs@NIPs was also inspected (Fig. 2E and F). The microstructural morphology of r/b-BCDs@NIPs was parallel to that of r/b-BCDs@MIPs, also displaying spherical nanoparticles that were mutually adhered, which was attributed to their similar synthesis process.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectrometer was used to analyze b-BCDs, r-BCDs, r/b-BCDs@MIPs, and r/b-BCDs@NIPs. The blue and red lines in Fig. 2G presented the FT-IR spectra of b-BCDs and r-BCDs, separately. In the range of 3100-3600 cm−1, there were distinguishing absorption broad peaks corresponding to the amino and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of biomass carbon dots (Sun et al., 2022). The characteristic absorption peaks near 2900 cm−1 corresponded to these methyl and methylene groups. The characteristic peaks around 1713 cm−1 were due to CO, while the characteristic peaks around 1050 cm−1 were due to the bending vibration of CO groups (Tao et al., 2022). The FT-IR spectra of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and r/b-BCDs@NIPs revealed the same characteristic peaks (Fig. 2H), situated at around 2950 cm−1, which were the vibrational peaks of methyl (-CH3) and methylene (-CH2) from EGDMA. The characteristic peaks at 1725 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 were owing to CO and CO, respectively, further attesting the foundation of the polymer (Han, Zhu, Zhang, Liu, & Sun, 2023). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also employed for structural analysis (Fig. S3AD). The XPS spectra of the b-BCDs presented typical peaks at 285.2, 399.9, and 532.3 eV (Fig. S3A), demonstrating that b-BCDs were primarily composed of C (60.42%), N (4.10%), and O (35.48%). Fig. S3B exhibited its high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, which has three typical peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and O-C=O, respectively. Fig. S3C displayed two absorption peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s, which agreed to NH (399.9 eV) and C-N-H (402.0 eV), correspondingly. The characteristic peaks in the XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. S3D) were positioned at 531.5, 532.4 and 533.2 eV, corresponding to O-C=O, CO and OH, respectively. The XPS spectra of r-BCDs presented that its chief integral elements were analogous to those of b-BCDs (Fig. S4A). The C1s spectra disclosed three typical peaks, corresponding to CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.2 eV), and O-C=O (288.7 eV), respectively (Fig. S4B). The characteristic peaks exhibited in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S4C) associated with the characteristic peaks of NH. The O1s spectra (Fig. S4D) had three characteristic peaks placed at 531.7, 532.7, and 533.5 eV, corresponding to O-C=O, CO, and OH, respectively (Ge et al., 2020). The XPS spectra of r/b-BCDs@MIPs were shown in Fig. S5AD. The r/b-BCDs@MIPs (Fig. S5A) had a higher proportion of C (71.27%) and O (27.94%), which was credited to the high C and O content in the synthesis materials. The C1s spectra (Fig. S5B) exhibited that there were three characteristic peaks corresponding to CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.4 eV), and O-C=O (288.7 eV), which could be assigned to EGDMA and MAA. The N1s spectra (Fig. S5C) showed a smaller characteristic peak located at 399.7 eV, which might be derivative of the BCDs, and the O1s spectra (Fig. S5D) exhibited three characteristic peaks located at 531.5, 532.5, and 533.4 eV, corresponding to O-C=O, CO, and OH, individually (Zhang et al., 2022; Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2022).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) indicated the analyses of b-BCDs, r-BCDs, and r/b-BCDs@MIPs. As exhibited in Fig. S6A, the two types of BCDs contained predominantly amorphous carbon structures due to the small size of the synthesized dots, and Fig. S6 B validated the XRD profile of the r/b-BCDs@MIPs, which presented a broad diffraction peak between 10 and 15°, signifying that the r/b-BCDs@MIPs were also amorphous structures (Hu et al., 2022). The BET surface areas of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and r/b-BCDs@NIPs were 27.5824 and 24.7334 m2/g, individually. The adsorption and desorption isotherms of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and r/b-BCDs@NIPs were showed in Fig. S7, individually.

To acquire the optimal detection ability, the solvent for detecting tyramine with r/b-BCDs@MIPs was primarily explored. As revealed in Fig. 3A, the MIPs materials presented diverse fluorescence intensities in water, methanol, ethanol, and acetonitrile, however, the fluorescence intensity in ethanol being significantly higher than that in other solvents. Thus, ethanol was chosen as the solvent for subsequent experiments. Adsorption time played a vital role for detection, and it was an imperative aspect in defining whether the detection results could reflect the actual results, so a dynamic adsorption experiment of r/b-BCDs@MIPs on tyramine was conducted, as displayed in Fig. 3B. After the addition of tyramine, the fluorescence signal of the MIPs material mixture system declined rapidly, after 11 min, ultimately attaining a stable plateau, and then there was practically no change. Thus, the optimal adsorption time of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was 27 min.

Subsequently, the detection ability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs for tyramine was explored by employing the optimal adsorption time. When mixed with varying concentrations of tyramine, the fluorescence emission peak of r/b-BCDs@MIPs situated at 670 nm was quenched, while the other fluorescence emission peak located at 460 nm displayed nearly no variation. Therefore, based on the above experimental data and phenomena, these results were calculated using the following equation:
(1)
F 670
/
F 460
= K sv
C
+ 1
where F 670 /F 460 represents the ratio of the fluorescence emission peaks of the MIPs material positioned at 670 nm and 460 nm, Ksv represents the fluorescence quenching constant, and C represents the concentration of tyramine standard solution. As presented in Fig. 3C, there was a good linear relationship between the concentration of F670/F460 and tyramine. After calculation, the linear equation was y = −0.00881× + 4.2265, R2 was 0.99108, and LOD was 1.3 μg/kg. For the purpose of control, the fluorescence response of tyramine solution to r/b-BCDs@NIPs under the same conditions was scrutinized (Fig. 3E and F). In the occurrence of different concentrations of tyramine, the fluorescence of r/b-BCDs@NIPs was also quenched, but the quenching effect was not apparent, with the quenching constant only being 0.00254, while the r/b-BCDs@MIPs was 0.00881, which was approximately 3.47 times that of r/b-BCDs@NIPs. It suggested that the adsorption detection effect of r/b-BCDs@MIPs for tyramine was significantly higher than that of the control group. This notable variance may be credited to the addition of tyramine as a template during the synthesis of r/b-BCDs@MIPs, which was removed through multiple solvent extractions at the end of synthesis, leaving a large number of three-dimensional cavities for the reabsorption of tyramine. During the synthesis of r/b-BCDs@NIPs, there was no significant number of three-dimensional cavities left, resulting in r/b-BCDs@MIPs having a stronger adsorption capacity for tyramine compared to the r/b-BCDs@NIPs. Meanwhile, we prepared r-BCDs@MIPs and b-BCDs@MIPs using the same procedure as r/b-BCDs@MIPs, with the exception that ethanol was used to replace the b-BCDs or r-BCDs. As shown in Fig. S8A, the fluorescence changes in r-BCDs@MIPs were obvious but the fluorescence changes in b-BCDs@MIPs were almost unchanged to the concentration of tyramine over the range of 1–60 μg/L. Fluorescence spectra of r-BCDs@MIPs and b-BCDs@MIPs in the presence of tyramine at various concentrations (1–60 μg/L) were shown in Fig. S8B. The LOD in the r-BCDs@MIPs system was 3.4 μg/kg, but it was 1.3 μg/kg in the r/b-BCDs@MIPs system. Therefore, the r/b-BCDs@MIPs was significantly improved detection sensitivity.

Additionally, to determine the selective recognition and adsorption ability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs for tyramine, selective adsorption experiments were carried out by using tyramine structural analogues of the same concentration (Fig. S9), such as tyrosine, tryptophan, tryptophan ester, etc., and r/b-BCDs@NIPs were adsorbed by the similar methodology. The outcomes were exhibited in Fig. S10A-C, in comparison with other compounds at the same concentration, r/b-BCDs@MIPs unveiled stronger fluorescence response towards tyramine, further demonstrating that r/b-BCDs@MIPs had a large number of three-dimensional cavities and higher adsorption ability towards tyramine. On the other hand, r/b-BCDs@NIPs had no notable disparity in fluorescence response towards tyramine and other structural analogues, because r/b-BCDs@NIP did not produce three-dimensional cavities with specific adsorption ability towards tyramine.

In addition, the competitive adsorption ability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was assessed by conducting 5-, 10-, and 15-fold competitive adsorption experiments with structural analogues (Fig. S10D and E). When high concentrations of structural analogues were added to the mixture of r/b-BCDs@MIPs and tyramine, the fluorescence response value was not significantly different from that of the control group with only tyramine (without adding high concentrations of structural analogues), representing that the three-dimensional cavities formed during the synthesis process of r/b-BCDs@MIPs had stronger adsorption ability for tyramine, and tyramine could entirely combine with these cavities, refining its explicit recognition ability.

To validate the anti-interference ability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs in the adsorption detection of tyramine, we also examined the impact of some potential interfering substances on the adsorption detection of tyramine employing r/b-BCDs@MIPs. In the interference experiment, ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, etc.) and amino acids (L-glutamic acid, L-cysteine, etc.) were used, and the concentration of these interfering substances in the mixed system was 1 mmol/L. As displayed in Fig. S10F and G, the occurrence of these interfering substances did not have a significant consequence on the fluorescence response value produced by r/b-BCDs@MIPs, signifying that the sensing system had a good anti-interference ability.

The experiment simulated the influence of high concentrations of ions on the fluorescence intensity of r/b-BCDs@MIPs by employing various varying concentrations of NaCl solution (Fig. S11A). With a steady increase in the concentration of NaCl, the fluorescence intensity of r/b-BCDs@MIPs remained comparatively stable without notable variations. Fluorescence stability was also estimated by continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp for 120 min, and the F670/F460 ratio in Fig. S11B remained fundamentally unaffected. From the results in Fig. S11C, it could be exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of r/b-BCDs@MIPs remained practically constant over a period of 30 days. All these results presented that r/b-BCDs@MIPs had robust fluorescence performance and stability. Moreover, the thermal stability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was also explored (Fig. S11D). The r/b-BCDs@MIPs presented favorable stability within the temperature range of 0–200 °C, with only trivial weight changes, which may be attributed to the release of residual solvents or adsorbed gases in MIPs at high temperatures. However, when the temperature reached 250 °C, a significant weight loss occurred, principally due to the progressive carbonization of organic compounds with high carbon content in the composition of r/b-BCDs@MIPs, resulting in structural disintegration and rapid weight loss. Finally, the repeated cycling adsorption capacity of r/b-BCDs@MIPs was evaluated as shown in Fig. S12, where three adsorption-desorption cycles were performed using 50 μg/L tyramine, and the r/b-BCDs@MIPs still had a good detection capacity for tyramine after three cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tyramine in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of r/b-BCDs@MIPs, three fermented meat products were selected as food samples for tyramine detection and spiked recovery experiments, and HPLC method was employed for authentication at three concentrations (5, 15, and 25 mg/kg) (Table 1 ). The results exhibited that the detection results based on r/b-BCDs@MIPs probe were consistent with HPLC (Fig. S13) results. At the same time, the method presented favorable recovery rates (79.74–112.12%) in spiked recovery experiments. Furthermore, these results confirmed the potential of the constructed probe for detecting tyramine in real samples. Table S1 presents an overview of the methods for detection of tyramine stated in recent literatures. The comparison with other detection methods revealed that, the method used in this study had low detection limits, good recovery rates, and stability.

The fluorescence signal changes induced by r/b-BCDs@MIPs after tyramine adsorption under 365 nm ultraviolet light were captured and analyzed using common smartphone software. As exhibited in Fig. 4A, the mixed system was transferred to a microtiter plate and located in a 365 nm UV dark box, and then examined by Spotxel® Reader. The analysis results displayed (Fig. 4B) that the concentration of tyramine standard solution had a commendable linear relationship with the fluorescence signal value (R2 = 0.97898), and the linear equation was y = −0.66796× + 145.3492, with the LOD of 8.28 μg/kg. A spiked recovery experiment employing a smartphone-based image analysis method to detect tyramine was also conducted, and the spiked recovery rates were ranging between 110.3 and 118.17% (Table S2), demonstrating that the smartphone-based image analysis method could interpret the content of tyramine in real samples.

In this study, two types of biomass carbon dots were manufactured by means of hydrothermal and solvothermal methods utilizing passion fruit shells, a biomass waste as the source. Remarkably, the fluorescence emission peaks of the two different types of carbon dots exhibited significant difference, positioned at blue and red wavelengths, correspondingly. A ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe was devised by integrating molecular imprinted technology employing these two different emission wavelengths of biomass carbon dots. One of the promising advantages of ratiometric fluorescence detection involves the effective minimization of background interference. Therefore, by integrating these both molecular imprinting technology and ratiometric fluorescence together the sensitivity and selectivity of the probe enhanced. The stability and practicability of the developed r/b-BCDs@MIPs had also been tested, yielding satisfactory experimental results. The constructed probe demonstrated satisfactory accuracy and low LOD in complex sample analysis. This was attributed to the stable reference signals provided by b-BCDs, which endowed r/b-BCDs@MIPs with superior self-calibration capability in comparison to single-signal fluorescence sensing. Moreover, a visual fluorescence sensing method integrated with smartphone analysis software was successfully established for the on-site detection of tyramine, which exhibited commendable recovery rate and accuracy in practical applications. The above results underscored that r/b-BCDs@MIPs had promising application potential for the detection and analysis of tyramine in fermented meat products.

Dianwei Zhang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Yuhua Zhang: Methodology. Kexin Li: Methodology. Shengnan Wang: Formal analysis. Yuanchen Ma: Methodology. Yonghong Liao: Project administration. Fenghuan Wang: Project administration. Huilin Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFF1101101) and the Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBU).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139759.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor based on metal-organic frameworks for sensitive detection of L-tyrosine
